does: This paper synthesizes existing literature on evaluating AI systems in healthcare by conducting a systematic search across three major databases for publications from 1970 onward, aiming to construct a minimally viable framework for assessing safety, acceptability, and efficacy. Rather than proposing novel methodology, the work consolidates empirical evidence and conceptual approaches from disparate research streams to identify core evaluation dimensions and criteria. The output is a structured framework that operationalizes how healthcare AI should be assessed across these three key domains, drawing on lessons from both successful implementations and failures in real-world clinical deployment.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in medical device regulation, clinical validation standards, and software quality assurance, while responding to recent acceleration in healthcare AI development that has outpaced governance infrastructure. It connects to parallel efforts in AI policy and regulatory frameworks emerging across jurisdictions, as well as clinical informatics research examining adoption barriers in hospital settings. The paper extends from medical ethics literature on acceptability and patient safety standards, while also drawing on biomedical engineering approaches to risk assessment and the growing field of algorithm auditing. It acknowledges that while technical AI safety research has advanced rapidly, healthcare has distinct requirements around interpretability, fairness, and regulatory accountability that demand domain-specific synthesis.
enables: By establishing a formalised evaluation framework, this work sets up clearer pathways for regulatory approval and clinical adoption of AI systems, potentially accelerating real-world deployment where systems can demonstrate conformance to standardized safety and efficacy criteria. The framework creates a common language across technologists, clinicians, regulators, and policymakers, enabling more effective coordination of AI validation efforts globally. It points toward development of specific validation protocols, benchmark datasets, and certification processes for healthcare AI. The synthesis also identifies remaining gaps where future research is needed, opening directions for operationalizing acceptability metrics, developing domain-specific safety testing procedures, and creating tools for ongoing post-deployment monitoring and evaluation of AI performance in clinical practice.
